title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter One: In which Sophie talks to hats (Fragment 3)
text:
Sophie could hardly say that she simply felt resigned to the hat trade. She thanked Fanny gratefully.

“So that’s settled then!” Fanny said.

The next day Sophie helped Martha pack her clothes in a box, and the morning after that they all saw her off on the carrier’s cart, looking small and upright and nervous. For the way to Upper Folding, where Mrs. Fairfax lived, lay over the hills past Wizard Howl’s moving castle. Martha was understandably scared.

“She’ll be all right,” said Lettie. Lettie refused all help with the packing. When the carrier’s cart was out of sight, Lettie crammed all her possessions into a pillow case and paid the neighbor’s bootboy sixpence to wheel it in a wheelbarrow to Cesari’s in Market Square. Lettie marched behind the wheelbarrow looking much more cheerful than Sophie expected. Indeed, she had the air of shaking the dust of the hat shop off her feet.

The bootboy brought back a scribbled note from Lettie, saying she had put her things in the girls’ dormitory and Cesari’s seemed great fun. A week later the carrier brought a letter from Martha to say that Martha had arrived safely and that Mrs. Fairfax was “a great dear and uses honey with everything. She keeps bees.” That was all Sophie heard of her sisters for quite a while, because she started her own apprenticeship the day Martha and Lettie left.

Sophie of course knew the hat trade quite well already. Since she was a tiny child she had run in and out of the big workshed across the yard where the hats were damped and molded on blocks, and flowers and fruit and other trimmings were made from wax and silk. She knew the people who worked there. Most of them had been there when her father was a boy. She knew Bessie, the only remaining shop assistant. She knew the customers who bought the hats and the man who drove the cart which fetched raw straw hats in from the country to be shaped on the blocks in the shed. She knew the other suppliers and how you made felt for winter hats. There was not really much that Fanny could teach her, except perhaps the best way to get a customer to buy a hat.

“You lead up to the right hat, love,” Fanny said. “Show them the ones that won’t quite do first, so they know the difference as soon as they put the right one on.”

In fact, Sophie did not sell hats very much. After a day or so observing in the workshed, and another day going round the clothier and the silk merchant’s with Fanny, Fanny set her to trimming hats. Sophie sat in a small alcove at the back of the shop, sewing roses to bonnets and veiling to velours, lining all of them with silk and arranging wax fruit and ribbons stylishly on the outsides. She was good at it. She quite liked doing it. But she felt isolated and a little dull. The workshop people were too old to be much fun and, besides, they treated her as someone apart who was going to inherit the business someday. Bessie treated her the same way. Bessie’s only talk anyway was about the farmer she was going to marry the week after May Day. Sophie rather envied Fanny, who could bustle off to bargain with the silk merchant whenever she wanted.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter One: In which Sophie talks to hats

vocabulary.definition: a small recessed section of a room
vocabulary.example: Sophie sat in a small alcove at the back of the shop.

vocabulary.definition: a period of training under a skilled employer
vocabulary.example: She started her own apprenticeship the day Martha and Lettie left.

vocabulary.definition: negotiate the terms of a transaction
vocabulary.example: Fanny could bustle off to bargain with the silk merchant.

vocabulary.definition: move energetically or noisily
vocabulary.example: Fanny could bustle off to bargain with the silk merchant.

vocabulary.definition: a person or thing that carries or conveys something
vocabulary.example: They all saw her off on the carrier’s cart.

vocabulary.definition: noticeably happy and optimistic
vocabulary.example: Lettie looked much more cheerful than Sophie expected.

vocabulary.definition: filled something completely and tightly
vocabulary.example: Lettie crammed all her possessions into a pillow case.

vocabulary.definition: made slightly wet
vocabulary.example: Where the hats were damped and molded on blocks.

vocabulary.definition: non-woven fabric made by matting fibers
vocabulary.example: You made felt for winter hats.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows thanks
vocabulary.example: She thanked Fanny gratefully.

vocabulary.definition: received something from a predecessor
vocabulary.example: She was going to inherit the business someday.

vocabulary.definition: separated from others
vocabulary.example: She felt isolated and a little dull.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with regular steps, often with purpose
vocabulary.example: Lettie marched behind the wheelbarrow looking much more cheerful.

vocabulary.definition: shaped into a particular form
vocabulary.example: Hats were molded on blocks.

vocabulary.definition: things owned by someone
vocabulary.example: Lettie crammed all her possessions into a pillow case.

vocabulary.definition: declined to accept or do something
vocabulary.example: Lettie refused all help with the packing.

vocabulary.definition: accepting something unpleasant because it seems inevitable
vocabulary.example: Sophie could hardly say that she simply felt resigned to the hat trade.

vocabulary.definition: written or drawn carelessly or hurriedly
vocabulary.example: The bootboy brought back a scribbled note from Lettie.

vocabulary.definition: decided or resolved
vocabulary.example: “So that’s settled then!” Fanny said.

vocabulary.definition: decorative additions to clothing or hats
vocabulary.example: Flowers and fruit and other trimmings were made from wax and silk.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that can be understood or accepted
vocabulary.example: Martha was understandably scared.

vocabulary.definition: standing straight up
vocabulary.example: Looking small and upright and nervous.

vocabulary.definition: a piece of thin material worn over the face or head
vocabulary.example: Sewing roses to bonnets and veiling to velours.

vocabulary.definition: a plush velvety fabric
vocabulary.example: Sewing veiling to velours and lining all of them with silk.

vocabulary.definition: a small cart with one wheel used to carry loads
vocabulary.example: She paid the neighbor’s bootboy sixpence to wheel it in a wheelbarrow.

vocabulary.definition: a shed or building where work is done
vocabulary.example: She had run in and out of the big workshed across the yard.
